title: Introduction
text: Whether you're working in the Browser or in Python, GDS workflows follow the same fundamental pattern. The steps are identical. The logic is identical. Only the syntax changes. This lesson walks through that workflow step by step, showing you how each piece translates from Cypher to Python. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-2-gds-workflows-python-s367-000-new] As you can see here, the diagram compares the GDS workflow steps in Cypher and Python, highlighting the identical logic and differing syntax.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to execute the standard five-step GDS workflow in Python. You'll learn to create graph projections and inspect them using the Graph object. You'll also be able to choose the right execution mode for different situations, work with algorithm results as pandas DataFrames, and clean up projections properly to manage memory.

title: The GDS Workflow
text: Every GDS analysis follows the same five steps: First, you load data into Neo4j if needed. Next, you project the graph into GDS memory. Then, you run algorithms on the projection. After that, you work with the results. Finally, you drop the projection. You did this in Modules 1 and 2 using Cypher. Now you'll do the same thing in Python. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-2-gds-workflows-python-s369-002-new] Notice how the flowchart illustrates the five-step GDS analysis process, from loading data to dropping the projection.

title: From Cypher to Python
text: In Module 2, you wrote Cypher projections like this: MATCH (source:User)-[r:P2P]->(target:User) WITH gds.graph.project('fraud-graph', source, target) AS g RETURN g.graphName, g.nodeCount. The Python equivalent uses the same concepts, but with a different interface. Let's work through each step.

title: Step 1: Loading Data
text: If your data isn't already in Neo4j, you can load it using `gds.run_cypher()`. This method executes any Cypher query and returns results as a pandas DataFrame. For example, you can load Movie nodes from a CSV file. For this workshop, the companion notebook handles data loading, but in practice, you'd often connect to an existing database.

title: Step 2: Creating Projections
text: The `gds.graph.project()` method returns two values: a Graph object and metadata about the projection. This example uses native projection syntax. In the next lesson, you'll learn how to translate your Cypher projection knowledge to native projection in Python.

title: The Graph Object
text: The Graph object, denoted as `G`, gives you methods to inspect your projection without querying the catalog directly. You can retrieve the graph name, node count, relationship count, node labels, relationship types, and properties available on nodes. You can also check memory consumption and verify if the graph exists in the catalog. These methods are useful for verifying your projection before running algorithms.

title: Step 3: Running Algorithms
text: Algorithm calls follow a consistent pattern. For example, to run degree centrality in mutate mode, you would use `gds.degree.mutate(G, mutateProperty="degree")`. The mode you choose determines what happens with the results.

title: The Four Execution Modes
text: Each mode serves a different purpose. The `.stream()` mode returns results as a DataFrame, ideal for analysis or visualization in Python. The `.mutate()` mode stores results in the projection only, useful when chaining multiple algorithms together. The `.write()` mode writes results back to Neo4j, which is helpful when you need to persist results for later queries. Lastly, the `.stats()` mode returns statistics only, suitable for quick checks without storing anything.

title: Stream Mode in Practice
text: Stream mode is the most common choice for analysis work. Results come back as a pandas DataFrame, allowing you to perform standard pandas operations immediately. For instance, you can find the top nodes by degree centrality. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-2-gds-workflows-python-s376-009-new] As illustrated here, data flows from a graph projection to a pandas DataFrame using stream mode, enabling data manipulation.

title: Step 4: Working with Results
text: Since stream mode returns DataFrames, you can use the full pandas toolkit. You can filter, sort, merge, and visualize data—whatever your analysis requires. For example, you can get degree centrality scores, find nodes above a certain threshold, and calculate summary statistics. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-2-gds-workflows-python-s377-010-new] This chart shows how to manipulate and analyze data using pandas operations on DataFrames returned from stream mode.

title: Step 5: Cleanup
text: Projections consume memory, so when you're finished with a projection, drop it. You can drop using the Graph object or the catalog. It's important to check what projections remain to avoid memory issues, especially in notebooks where you might create multiple projections during exploration. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-2-gds-workflows-python-s378-011-new] This flowchart illustrates the cleanup process for projections, including dropping projections using the Graph object and catalog.

title: The Context Manager Pattern
text: Python's `with` statement provides automatic cleanup. When the block ends, the projection is dropped—even if an error occurs. This pattern is especially useful for exploratory work where you're creating and discarding projections frequently.

title: Putting It Together
text: Here's the complete workflow in one place. You start by connecting to the database using `GraphDataScience`. Then, you project the graph, run an algorithm, work with the results, and finally, clean up by dropping the projection and closing the connection. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-2-gds-workflows-python-s380-013-new] This flowchart depicts the complete GDS workflow in Python, from connecting to the database to projecting, running algorithms, analyzing, and cleaning up.

title: Summary
text: The GDS workflow in Python mirrors what you learned in Cypher. The `gds.graph.project()` method returns a Graph object for inspecting projections. You have four execution modes to choose from: `.stream()`, `.mutate()`, `.write()`, and `.stats()`. Always drop projections when finished—or use context managers for automatic cleanup. Remember to include `defaultValue` when projecting properties to handle nulls. In the companion notebook, you'll work through each step hands-on with the Movies dataset. Next, we'll explore understanding projection syntax options—native vs. Cypher projection in Python.
